items.q: How does the rate of an SN2 reaction depend on concentrations?
items.correct: Rate is first order in both substrate AND nucleophile (overall second order) because both are involved in the rate-determining step.
items.misconception: SN2 reactions are first order overall because only the substrate matters.
items.random_wrong: SN2 reactions are zero order because they happen in one step.
items.why: Students confuse SN1 (rate depends only on substrate) and SN2 (rate depends on both); the '2' in SN2 refers to the order, but they forget which way round.

items.q: What does an NMR chemical shift (δ, in ppm) actually measure?
items.correct: The position of a peak RELATIVE to TMS (tetramethylsilane, δ = 0), normalised by the spectrometer frequency.
items.misconception: Chemical shift is the absolute resonance frequency of the nucleus in Hz.
items.random_wrong: Chemical shift is the mass of the molecule divided by 1000.
items.why: Students see ppm and think it's an absolute scale, not realising it's a frequency-independent ratio against a reference.

items.q: What are the units of Kc for a reaction?
items.correct: They depend on Δn (the difference between moles of gaseous/aqueous products and reactants) — Kc can be dimensionless or have units like mol dm^-3.
items.misconception: Kc always has units of mol dm^-3.
items.why: Students learn Kc with a fixed unit example and don't realise units arise from the exponents in the expression; for A + B ⇌ C + D, Kc is unitless, but for A ⇌ B + C, Kc has units.
Correct answer: Constant. “Decreasing” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: First-order reaction with k = 0.0693 s⁻¹. Half-life in seconds:
items.why: The real figure is 10. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
